patterns.name: Experiential Learning Cycle
patterns.summary: Learning advances through a four-stage cycle: concrete experience, reflective observation, abstract conceptualization, and active experimentation. Knowledge is created not by absorbing information but by transforming lived experience through systematic reflection and deliberate testing. Skipping any stage leaves the cycle incomplete. Acting without reflecting, or theorising without testing, makes learning shallow.
patterns.intent: Deepen learning by cycling continuously through doing, reflecting, concluding, and experimenting rather than treating any single stage as sufficient.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever a learner has direct access to a real task or situation and enough time for structured reflection. Use it at the start of a skill-building initiative to set the repeating rhythm. Return to it whenever learning stalls because the learner is stuck in one stage. That stall might be over-theorising without acting, or acting without reflecting.

inputs.name: Concrete experience
inputs.description: A real activity, task, simulation, or encounter that the learner performs — not a description read about, but something directly lived or done.

inputs.name: Reflective capacity and time
inputs.description: Structured opportunity and prompts for the learner to observe and review what happened, without rushing immediately to conclusions or next actions.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has moved through at least one full cycle and can name what they experienced, what they observed, what principle they formed, and what they will test next.

outputs.name: Tested abstraction
outputs.description: A generalised principle, model, or rule that the learner formed from reflection and has then applied experimentally — the masterpiece of one cycle, and the input to the next.

steps.name: Concrete experience
steps.description: The learner carries out a real task, experiment, or encounter. The experience must be genuine — not hypothetical — because the learning derives from what actually happened, including surprises and failures.

steps.name: Reflective observation
steps.description: The learner pauses and reviews the experience from multiple angles: what happened, what was noticed, what felt unexpected, what others might have seen differently. Reflection is structured, not casual — journal prompts, guided questions, or peer discussion create the discipline.

steps.name: Abstract conceptualisation
steps.description: The learner draws conclusions, forms a principle, or builds a mental model that makes sense of the observations. This is the generalisation step: from 'what happened here' to 'what this means in general.'

steps.name: Active experimentation
steps.description: The learner applies the new principle to a fresh situation — a new task, a variation, or a real problem. The experiment generates a new concrete experience, restarting the cycle at a higher level of understanding.

principles: Knowledge is created through transformation of experience, not through transmission of information.
All four stages are necessary — a cycle that stops at reflection produces insight without skill; one that stops at action produces habit without understanding.
Each completed cycle raises the entry point of the next — learning is genuinely cumulative.
patterns.threshold: The learner can walk through a completed cycle from memory — naming the concrete experience, the observation, the principle formed, and the experiment conducted — and can identify where in a current cycle they are stuck.
patterns.masterpiece: A portfolio of completed cycles in which each active-experimentation stage generated a new concrete experience that became the input to the following cycle — showing genuine cumulative growth rather than repeated entry-level encounters.

known_uses.name: Medical and nursing education
known_uses.note: Kolb's cycle used as the structural framework for clinical rotation design, simulation debriefs, and reflective journals in health professions education.

known_uses.name: Reinforcement-learning agent training
known_uses.note: The four-stage structure maps onto RL agent loops: environment interaction (CE), reward analysis (RO), policy update (AC), next-episode execution (AE).

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: jumping from concrete experience directly to the next action without reflective observation or conceptualisation. The cycle completes fast but no generalisation forms — the learner repeats the same experience repeatedly without advancing.

Anti-pattern: stopping after abstract conceptualisation and never running the active-experimentation step. The learner accumulates untested models that feel certain but have not been stress-tested against reality.

sources.title: Experiential Learning: Experience as the Source of Learning and Development
sources.quote: Learning is the process whereby knowledge is created through the transformation of experience.
sources.note: Kolb, D.A. (1984), p. 38. Quote confirmed by multiple secondary academic sources citing the original.

sources.title: Kolb's Learning Styles and Experiential Learning Cycle — Simply Psychology
sources.quote: Effective learning is seen when a person progresses through a cycle of four stages: of (1) having a concrete experience followed by (2) observation of and reflection on that experience which leads to (3) the formation of abstract concepts (analysis) and generalizations (conclusions) which are then (4) used to test a hypothesis in future situations, resulting in new experiences.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Concrete Experience
Do the real thing] --> B[Reflective Observation
Review what happened]
    B --> C[Abstract Conceptualisation
Form a principle or model]
    C --> D[Active Experimentation
Apply the principle to a new situation]
    D --> E[New Concrete Experience
higher-level entry point]
    E --> B

patterns.example_scenario: A software engineer joins a new codebase and is asked to fix a bug she has never seen before. She attempts the fix (concrete experience) — it partially works but introduces a regression. She pauses and reviews her approach: she had changed a shared utility without tracing all its callers (reflective observation). She forms a principle: shared utilities must be traced before any modification (abstract conceptualisation). On the next bug fix she writes a caller-trace script first, runs it, and only then edits the utility (active experimentation). The script surfaces two callers she would have missed. The new experience is running the trace, seeing the callers, and making the safe edit. It becomes the entry to a new cycle. In that cycle she reflects on when caller-tracing adds most value. After three cycles, she has a reliable, tested practice rather than a one-off workaround.

patterns.name: Learning by Doing
patterns.summary: Learning happens through purposeful action on real problems, not through passive reception of information. The learner is given something to do — not something to learn — and the doing demands thinking. Education connects to students' genuine interests so that success in the activity depends on developing the skill, making learning intrinsically motivated rather than externally rewarded. Associated with John Dewey, grounded in his pragmatist philosophy of education.
patterns.intent: Produce genuine learning by immersing the learner in purposeful activity on a real problem where thinking is required and success is visible.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner needs a skill that can only be internalised through practice — not when knowledge transfer alone is sufficient. Also apply when motivation is low and abstract instruction has stalled: connecting the skill to a real problem the learner cares about restarts engagement. Most powerful when the learner can see a direct link between their action and the outcome.

inputs.name: Real task or problem
inputs.description: A genuine activity that requires the target skill to succeed — not a drill designed around the skill in isolation, but a meaningful task where the skill serves a purpose the learner can see.

inputs.name: Learner with a stake in the outcome
inputs.description: A learner who has some interest in solving the problem — however small — so that success in the activity creates an intrinsic reason to develop the skill.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has developed the skill through doing rather than hearing about it — with tactile understanding of where the skill is difficult and where it is effective.

outputs.name: Completed work product
outputs.description: A real artefact produced by the learner through the activity — the masterpiece — which demonstrates the skill and anchors the learning to a concrete achievement rather than an abstract certificate.

steps.name: Select a real task that requires the skill
steps.description: Choose or design a genuine problem where the learner must use the target skill to make progress. Avoid synthetic exercises that simulate the skill in isolation — the task should have real stakes, even small ones, so that thinking is genuinely required.

steps.name: Place the learner in the task
steps.description: Give the learner primary responsibility for doing the work. The instructor's role shifts from information source to environment designer and supportive observer. Resist the urge to tell the learner what to do before they have tried.

steps.name: Let difficulty surface and provide targeted support
steps.description: Allow the learner to encounter obstacles in the task. Intervene with just enough support to keep them productive — not so much that you remove the difficulty, which is the source of learning. The difficulty is not a problem to eliminate; it is the mechanism.

steps.name: Connect the action to the principle
steps.description: After the learner has done enough to form an experience, surface the underlying principle together. 'You succeeded here because you traced the data through each stage — that is what the skill of data lineage tracing means in practice.' The principle arrives after the doing, not before.

principles: Give learners something to do, not something to learn — the doing is what generates the learning.
Thinking is demanded by meaningful activity, not by instruction — choose tasks where success depends on genuine reasoning.
The principle is anchored by the experience — explain it after the learner has encountered it, not before.
patterns.threshold: The learner can use the skill on a new problem of similar type without instruction, and can explain why the skill works by reference to their own experience of using it.
patterns.masterpiece: A real completed work product — built, written, debugged, or designed — that the learner produced by doing, which they can point to as evidence of the skill and from which they can reconstruct the principles they used.

known_uses.name: Software coding bootcamps
known_uses.org: Various (Recurse Center, App Academy, Turing School)
known_uses.note: Project-based curriculum in which learners build real applications from week one, developing skills through direct encounter with real problems rather than through lecture sequences.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent capability acquisition
known_uses.note: Agents trained by executing real tasks in live environments rather than by imitation of recorded behaviour — performance on real tasks, not supervised copying, drives capability growth.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: activity without any structured pause for review or sense-making. The learner accumulates task experience but forms no transferable understanding — they can repeat what they did but cannot apply the skill to a novel situation.

Anti-pattern: dropping the learner into a task that exceeds their current capacity with no support, expecting struggle alone to produce learning. Productive struggle requires enough scaffolding that the learner can make progress; pure overwhelm produces discouragement, not growth.

sources.title: Democracy and Education — John Dewey (1916)
sources.quote: They give the pupils something to do, not something to learn; and the doing is of such a nature as to demand thinking, or the intentional noting of connections; learning naturally results.
sources.note: Dewey, J. (1916). Democracy and Education. New York: Macmillan. Core statement of the learn-by-doing principle.

sources.title: Experience and Education — John Dewey (1938)
sources.quote: Every experience enacted and undergone modifies the one who acts and undergoes, while this modification affects...the quality of subsequent experiences.
sources.note: Dewey, J. (1938). Experience and Education. New York: Macmillan. p. 35. Confirms the transformative role of action in learning.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Real task requiring the skill
with genuine stakes] --> B[Learner takes primary responsibility
instructor observes]
    B --> C[Difficulty surfaces
obstacles encountered]
    C --> D[Targeted support
just enough to stay productive]
    D --> E[Learner acts through the difficulty
skill applied in context]
    E --> F[Connect action to principle
after the doing, not before]
    F --> G[More capable learner
+ completed work product]

patterns.example_scenario: A data analyst has been told in several workshops about the importance of checking data distributions before fitting a regression model. She understands the advice but does not feel it as urgent. Her manager gives her a real business question: predict next quarter's sales using last year's transaction data. She builds the model without checking distributions and presents results that are off by 40%. She investigates and finds the transaction data is heavily right-skewed by a single outlier quarter. She transforms the data, rebuilds the model, and the predictions become usable. The principle — check distributions before modelling — is now attached to a real failure she can name. In every subsequent project she checks distributions first, without being reminded, because the doing made the principle tangible in a way no lecture had. The masterpiece is the rebuilt model and the documented investigation: a real artefact that she can point to as the origin of the practice.

patterns.name: Guided Discovery Learning
patterns.summary: The instructor designs an environment where learners explore and find patterns themselves, rather than being told conclusions. Scaffolding keeps exploration productive without removing the discovery. The teacher asks guiding questions, provides structured materials, breaks goals into smaller steps, and highlights key points. But the learner does the inferring. Jerome Bruner argued that knowledge discovered through one's own exploration is retained better and transferred more readily than knowledge received by instruction. The instructor is essential; this is not free play.
patterns.intent: Build durable, transferable knowledge by letting learners discover structure themselves within a carefully designed and scaffolded environment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the target knowledge has underlying structure the learner can detect through examples. Apply when the goal is transfer to novel situations rather than recall. Apply when the learner's motivation benefits from agency. Do not apply when precise procedural compliance is required immediately. Discovery learning is slower and requires tolerance for productive error.

inputs.name: Designed exploratory environment
inputs.description: Materials, problems, cases, or data arranged so that the target structure is discoverable — the learner can find it by exploring, but it is not handed to them explicitly.

inputs.name: Scaffolding plan
inputs.description: A set of guiding questions, step-down hints, examples, and encouragement the instructor will deploy as the learner encounters difficulty — enough support to keep exploration productive, not so much that the discovery is made for the learner.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has discovered a principle, pattern, or structure themselves and can therefore apply it to unfamiliar variations — not just recall it in the form it was first encountered.

outputs.name: Self-discovered principle or structure
outputs.description: A generalisation the learner articulated themselves through exploration — the masterpiece — which they own because they found it, making it both more memorable and more transferable.

steps.name: Design the discovery environment
steps.description: Select or create materials, problems, or cases in which the target structure is present and detectable. Sequence them so that simpler cases make the pattern visible before more complex cases test it. The instructor's design work is the hardest part of this move.

steps.name: Launch exploration with a clear but open question
steps.description: Give the learner a genuine question to explore — specific enough to focus inquiry, open enough that the answer must be found rather than looked up. The question should make the learner curious, not anxious.

steps.name: Scaffold without revealing
steps.description: As the learner explores, provide guidance through questions and hints rather than answers. If the learner is stuck, ask what they notice, what is similar to something they already know, or what would happen if they tried a variation. Model thinking-out-loud, not conclusions.

steps.name: Surface and consolidate the discovery
steps.description: When the learner names a pattern or principle themselves, help them articulate it precisely and test it against additional cases. The consolidation step prevents the discovery from remaining a vague feeling — it becomes a named, testable claim the learner owns.

principles: The instructor is essential — guided discovery is not free exploration; the design of the environment and the scaffolding are active teaching acts.
Discovery that the learner makes themselves transfers better than knowledge the instructor states — the act of finding it is part of what makes it stick.
Productive struggle is the mechanism — the learner must be allowed to be confused before the pattern becomes clear; removing confusion too early removes the learning.
patterns.threshold: The learner can apply the discovered principle to a new problem type not covered in the original exploration, without scaffolding — demonstrating transfer, not just recall.
patterns.masterpiece: A self-articulated principle or model, stated in the learner's own words, that the learner tested against at least two novel cases they had not encountered during the original exploration — evidence of genuine discovery rather than surface pattern-matching.

known_uses.name: LOGO programming for children
known_uses.note: Papert's LOGO environment applied Bruner-style guided discovery: children explored turtle geometry and discovered mathematical principles through programming, not through instruction.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent curriculum design
known_uses.note: Structured task progressions in agent training where harder sub-goals are only accessible after the agent has discovered prerequisite behaviours — scaffolded exploration at the curriculum level.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: removing all instructor guidance and letting the learner explore without structure. Pure unguided discovery is less effective than direct instruction for most learners — the 'guide' in guided discovery is load-bearing.

Anti-pattern: the instructor's scaffolding escalates to directly revealing the answer when the learner struggles, replacing the discovery with instruction. The principle is discovered for the learner rather than by the learner, removing the transfer benefit.

sources.title: The Act of Discovery — Jerome S. Bruner (1961)
sources.note: Bruner, J.S. (1961). The Act of Discovery. Harvard Educational Review, 31, 21–32. Foundational paper establishing the value of learner-driven discovery over direct instruction.

sources.title: Toward a Theory of Instruction — Jerome S. Bruner (1966)
sources.quote: students should discover for themselves the structure of the subject they are studying
sources.note: Bruner, J.S. (1966). Toward a Theory of Instruction. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. Paraphrase of core claim confirmed by multiple secondary sources; direct quote from simplypsychology.org secondary synthesis.

sources.title: Jerome Bruner Theory of Cognitive Development — Simply Psychology
sources.note: Bruner's phrase for the goal of discovery learning: learners should be able to extrapolate beyond what was explicitly presented. Confirmed as direct Bruner phrasing by Simply Psychology synthesis.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Instructor designs
exploration environment] --> B[Open question posed
to learner]
    B --> C[Learner explores
cases and materials]
    C --> D{Pattern noticed?}
    D -- No --> E[Instructor scaffolds
question / hint / variation]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Learner articulates principle
in own words]
    F --> G[Test against novel cases
not in original set]
    G --> H[More capable learner
+ self-discovered principle]

patterns.example_scenario: A data science instructor wants a group of junior analysts to understand why log-transforming skewed data before regression improves model fit. Rather than explaining this, she prepares three datasets: one normally distributed, one moderately skewed, one severely skewed, all with the same underlying linear relationship to the target. She asks the group to fit a linear model to each and report their R-squared values. The models on the skewed datasets perform poorly. She asks: 'What is different about those datasets? Try transforming the skewed variable — you choose how — and refit.' The group tries square root and log transforms. Log transformation on the severe skew dataset jumps R-squared from 0.31 to 0.79. The instructor asks them what they changed and why they think it helped. The group articulates the principle themselves: when a variable spans several orders of magnitude, regression on the raw scale is dominated by extreme values; compressing the scale makes the linear relationship visible. They now own this principle through discovery. On a new dataset two weeks later they apply log transformation without being prompted.

patterns.name: Reflective Practice
patterns.summary: Expertise deepens through two kinds of reflection: thinking-in-action (adjusting mid-performance as something unexpected arises) and thinking-on-action (deliberate review after performance to name what happened and why). Donald Schön showed that competent professionals routinely know more than they can say — their tacit knowledge is revealed through reflection, not through formal learning. Reflective practice is the discipline of making that tacit knowledge explicit and revisable.
patterns.intent: Surface and revise the tacit knowledge driving professional performance by reflecting both during and after action.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever a practitioner encounters a surprising outcome, a situation that resists standard techniques, or a moment where their intuitive response was wrong. Also apply on a scheduled basis — not just when things go wrong — to surface the knowledge that successful performance relies on and to check whether it still holds in changing conditions.

inputs.name: Performance event with surprise or friction
inputs.description: A professional action, decision, or encounter that produced an unexpected outcome, required improvisation, or revealed a mismatch between what the practitioner expected and what happened.

inputs.name: Structured reflection opportunity
inputs.description: Dedicated time and a method — journal, peer conversation, structured questions — for the practitioner to revisit the event without rushing to the next task.

outputs.name: More capable practitioner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has surfaced previously tacit knowledge, named it, and tested whether it is still valid — able to revise their practice rather than simply accumulating experience.

outputs.name: Named and revisable practice principle
outputs.description: A stated principle, reframing, or updated model — the masterpiece — derived from the reflection, which the practitioner can now apply deliberately rather than relying on implicit habit.

steps.name: Notice the surprise or friction
steps.description: During or after a performance event, identify the moment where something did not go as expected, where standard technique did not fit, or where an intuitive response turned out to be wrong. The surprise is the entry point for reflection.

steps.name: Reflect in the moment (reflection-in-action)
steps.description: While still in the situation, the practitioner consciously considers what is happening, questions their initial framing, and tries an adjusted approach. This is not systematic analysis — it is a real-time questioning of knowing-in-action while there is still opportunity to act.

steps.name: Reflect after the event (reflection-on-action)
steps.description: After the performance event, the practitioner reviews what happened: what they noticed, what they assumed, what they tried, what resulted. The goal is not to justify the past action but to name the knowledge that was driving it and ask whether that knowledge should change.

steps.name: Articulate and test the revised principle
steps.description: State the revised understanding explicitly — 'I used to think X, now I think Y because of Z' — and identify a near-term opportunity to test it. Untested revisions remain hypotheses; tested revisions become updated practice.

principles: Competent practitioners know more than they can say — reflection is the discipline that surfaces tacit knowledge so it can be named and revised.
Reflection-in-action is not the same as reflection-on-action — both are necessary; the first adjusts current performance, the second upgrades future practice.
Surprise is the invitation — the moment where standard technique does not fit is where learning is available.
patterns.threshold: The practitioner can name a principle that was driving their intuitive performance, describe the event that surfaced it, and explain what changed in their practice as a result of reflecting on it.
patterns.masterpiece: A collection of named, dated practice revisions — each tied to a specific performance event, stating the prior assumption, the surprise that questioned it, and the updated principle — demonstrating that practice is genuinely evolving rather than merely accumulating.

known_uses.name: Medical education and clinical training
known_uses.note: Reflective journals and structured debrief after clinical encounters are a standard component of residency and continuing professional development programmes.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent self-review loops
known_uses.note: LLM-based agent designs that include mid-task re-examination of reasoning (analogous to reflection-in-action) and post-task structured review (analogous to reflection-on-action) to improve future performance.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: completing a reflection ritual — filling in a journal template, attending a debrief — without identifying any actual change in understanding or practice. Reflection that produces no named revision is performance compliance, not learning.

Anti-pattern: triggering structured reflection only when something goes wrong, missing the tacit knowledge embedded in successful performance. Effective practices that work for unclear reasons are the hardest to preserve when conditions change — they need reflection too.

sources.title: The Reflective Practitioner: How Professionals Think in Action — Donald A. Schön (1983)
sources.quote: Practitioners themselves often reveal a capacity for reflection on their intuitive knowing in the midst of action.
sources.note: Schön, D.A. (1983). The Reflective Practitioner. New York: Basic Books. p. 8. Quote confirmed via book-notes secondary source citing the original.

sources.title: The Reflective Practitioner — Donald A. Schön (1983)
sources.quote: competent practitioners usually know more than they can say
sources.note: Schön, D.A. (1983). The Reflective Practitioner. New York: Basic Books. p. 8. Core claim about tacit knowledge.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Performance event begins] --> B{Surprise or friction?}
    B -- Yes, mid-action --> C[Reflection-in-action
question assumptions, try adjustment]
    C --> D[Continue performance
with adjusted approach]
    D --> E[Event concludes]
    B -- No --> E
    E --> F[Reflection-on-action
structured review after event]
    F --> G[Name tacit knowledge
that was driving performance]
    G --> H[Articulate revised principle
test in next situation]
    H --> I[More capable practitioner
+ named practice revision]

patterns.example_scenario: A senior product manager runs a quarterly roadmap planning session. She has a well-practised approach: open with priorities, walk through capacity, assign stories. In this session an engineering lead keeps raising dependency concerns that disrupt the flow. Her standard facilitation does not settle the room; tension escalates. She notices this mid-session (reflection-in-action) and shifts: she stops the priority walkthrough and asks the engineering lead to spend ten minutes mapping the dependencies on a whiteboard before continuing. The room stabilises. After the session she reflects on what happened (reflection-on-action): her standard approach assumed capacity constraints were the main source of difficulty; this team's difficulty was uncertainty about sequencing. She revises her opening move for teams with shared-component work: dependency mapping before capacity allocation. She names and writes the revision. In the next planning session with a similarly structured team, she starts with dependency mapping and the session runs smoothly. The masterpiece is the written revision: prior assumption named, surprise event described, updated practice stated, tested in the next session.

patterns.name: Experimental Exploration with Checkpoints
patterns.summary: When a problem is too uncertain to plan directly, the learner runs a time-boxed experiment — a spike — aimed at a specific question rather than a deliverable. A fixed time limit and a clearly stated question keep the exploration productive without allowing it to become unbounded research. Checkpoints at the midpoint and end of the box force a decision: is the question answered, should the box be extended, or should the approach change? The output is knowledge, not code or a product — and that knowledge is sufficient to unlock the next planning decision.
patterns.intent: Resolve a specific uncertainty through a strictly time-boxed exploration so that the next planning or learning decision can be made on evidence rather than assumption.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a decision or next step is blocked by a specific unknown — a technical question, a feasibility concern, a conceptual gap — that can be investigated in a bounded time without full commitment to an implementation. Do not apply to vague uncertainty; the exploration requires a concrete, answerable question. Do not extend the box without a conscious checkpoint decision.

inputs.name: Specific answerable question
inputs.description: A precise question whose answer will unblock a decision — not 'how does this technology work?' but 'can this library handle 10,000 concurrent connections within our latency budget?'

inputs.name: Fixed time box
inputs.description: A hard upper bound on exploration time, agreed before the spike begins — typically one to three days. The box is the guardrail that prevents learning from becoming indefinite research.

outputs.name: More capable learner or team
outputs.description: A learner or team that has resolved a concrete uncertainty and can now make the next decision based on evidence rather than assumption.

outputs.name: Spike report
outputs.description: A short written record — the masterpiece — stating the question explored, the approach taken, what was found, and the decision it now enables. The spike report is what the learning produced; any code or prototype is disposable unless explicitly retained.

steps.name: State the question and the time box
steps.description: Before beginning, write the specific question the spike must answer and the hard end time of the box. Share both with anyone affected by the next decision. A spike without a written question tends to drift into general research.

steps.name: Run the exploration
steps.description: Investigate the question using the fastest reliable method — prototype, literature scan, expert interview, experiment, or calculation. Explore only what serves the question; resist the pull of interesting adjacent territory.

steps.name: Midpoint checkpoint
steps.description: At the midpoint of the time box, assess: is the question going to be answered by the end? If yes, continue. If no, decide: narrow the question further, extend the box with a specific reason, or stop and report what was learned so far. The checkpoint is mandatory — an unchecked spike will always expand.

steps.name: Write the spike report
steps.description: At the end of the box, write the spike report: question asked, approach taken, what was found, and what decision is now enabled. If the question was not fully answered, say so and state what further investigation would be needed. The report is the output — not the prototype.

principles: The output is knowledge, not a deliverable — any artefact produced during the spike is a means of learning, not the goal.
The time box is not a suggestion — it is the mechanism that distinguishes a spike from open-ended research; a soft box is no box.
A concrete question before entry, a written report at exit — these are the two non-negotiable boundaries of the move.
patterns.threshold: The learner can identify the specific decision that the spike unblocked and explain, with reference to the spike report, what evidence changed the situation from uncertain to decided.
patterns.masterpiece: A spike report that states a precise question, a bounded investigation, a clear finding, and the decision it enabled — demonstrable by showing that the next planning step was made using the spike's evidence rather than assumption.

known_uses.name: Extreme Programming spike solutions
known_uses.quote: Spike is an Extreme Programming term meaning experiment. We use the word because we think of a spike as a quick, almost brute-force experiment aimed at learning just one thing.
known_uses.note: Ron Jeffries, XP co-originator, defining spikes as learning experiments. Quote sourced from johnclapham.wordpress.com citing Jeffries directly.

known_uses.name: Agile team uncertainty management
known_uses.quote: a spike refers to a time-boxed research activity that helps teams make better decisions and deliver better products
known_uses.note: Mountain Goat Software definition of spike in agile practice.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent exploration tasks
known_uses.note: Agent design patterns that include bounded exploration sub-tasks with a hard step limit and a written finding before proceeding — spike structure applied to agent planning loops.

known_uses.name: Spikes (Scaled Agile Framework)
known_uses.quote: When faced with a question, risk, or uncertainty, Agile Teams conduct small experiments before moving to implementation rather than speculate about the outcome.
known_uses.note: SAFe codifies the spike at enterprise scale, framing the deliverable explicitly as risk reduction — gaining the knowledge needed to reduce the risk of a technical approach or improve estimate reliability.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: starting an exploration with a vague question and no hard time box, calling it a spike. Without a concrete question and a fixed end time, the activity is general research — useful, but not the move. Learning is diffuse and no decision is unblocked.

Anti-pattern: retaining and building on the prototype, script, or code produced during the spike as if it were production-quality. Spike artefacts are disposable learning tools; treating them as deliverables introduces technical debt built on exploratory, unreviewed work.

patterns.ecosystem: software engineering, professional development, autonomous-agent training

sources.title: Why is a Spike called a Spike? — John Clapham (2016)
sources.quote: Spike is an Extreme Programming term meaning experiment. We use the word because we think of a spike as a quick, almost brute-force experiment aimed at learning just one thing.
sources.note: Ron Jeffries (XP co-originator) quoted directly. Confirms XP origin and 'learning one thing' framing.

sources.title: What Are Agile Spikes? — Mountain Goat Software
sources.quote: a spike refers to a time-boxed research activity that helps teams make better decisions and deliver better products
sources.note: Mike Cohn / Mountain Goat Software secondary definition confirming time-boxed and decision-enabling framing.

sources.title: Spikes — Scaled Agile Framework
sources.quote: Spikes represent activities such as exploration, architecture, infrastructure, research, design, and prototyping.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Specific answerable question
+ hard time box agreed] --> B[Run exploration
fastest reliable method]
    B --> C[Midpoint checkpoint
will question be answered?]
    C -- Yes --> D[Continue exploration]
    C -- No: too broad --> E[Narrow question
or extend box deliberately]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Time box ends]
    F --> G[Write spike report
question / approach / finding / decision enabled]
    G --> H[More capable learner
+ decision unblocked by evidence]

patterns.example_scenario: A machine learning team is deciding whether to switch their inference pipeline from batch processing to real-time streaming. The architecture change is large; committing to it without evidence would be expensive if the latency targets cannot be met. The team proposes a spike: the question is 'Can our current model serve predictions at under 200ms p99 latency with 500 concurrent users on our candidate streaming framework?' The time box is two days. One engineer runs the investigation: deploys a minimal version of the model behind the streaming framework, generates load at 500 concurrent users, and measures latency. At the midpoint checkpoint the question is on track. At the end of the box she writes the spike report: p99 latency was 147ms under load, within budget, but memory overhead was 40% higher than batch. Decision enabled: proceed with streaming architecture, but include a memory monitoring alert in the implementation plan. The report is shared; the team makes the architecture decision in the next planning session based on evidence. No code from the spike is retained — it was exploratory scaffolding. The masterpiece is the spike report: a two-page document that replaced weeks of assumption-based planning.

patterns.name: After-Action Review
patterns.summary: A structured facilitated discussion held immediately after a significant event, in which participants — not the facilitator — discover for themselves what happened, why it happened, and what to sustain or change. Developed by the US Army following analysis of the 1973 Yom Kippur War and institutionalised at the National Training Center from 1981. The facilitator guides through questions; rank is suspended; conclusions are owned by participants, not imposed by leaders. AARs are not critiques — they do not assign blame or determine winners and losers.
patterns.intent: Extract transferable lessons from a completed event by guiding all participants to discover — through structured questioning — what happened, why it happened, and how performance should change.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply immediately after any significant event, exercise, project milestone, or failure where performance is worth understanding — while details are fresh and participants can still recall their decisions and reasoning. Also apply after successes: the discipline of asking why something went well is as valuable as asking why it went wrong, and less common.

inputs.name: Completed event with participants
inputs.description: A training exercise, operation, project milestone, product launch, incident, or any significant shared activity that has recently concluded, with the people who participated available to reflect.

inputs.name: Facilitator with the four AAR questions
inputs.description: A facilitator (not necessarily a leader) who guides the discussion through the four questions without imposing conclusions. The facilitator's role is to draw out participant discovery, not to deliver a verdict.

outputs.name: More capable team
outputs.description: A team that understands what drove its performance in the event — both strengths to sustain and gaps to close — and has shared ownership of the lessons because participants discovered them together.

outputs.name: Lessons-learned record
outputs.description: A written record — the masterpiece — of what was planned, what happened, what caused the gap, and what the team will sustain or improve — actionable enough to inform the next similar event.

steps.name: Review what was planned
steps.description: Ask: 'What were we trying to do? What was the intent?' Establish a shared baseline of the original plan, goals, and expected outcomes — without which the gap between plan and reality cannot be seen clearly.

steps.name: Establish what actually happened
steps.description: Ask: 'What actually happened?' Reconstruct the sequence of events as participants experienced it, not as leaders hoped it went. Use multiple perspectives — different participants saw different things. Avoid judgment at this stage; the goal is an accurate picture.

steps.name: Determine why the gap exists
steps.description: Ask: 'Why was there a difference between what was planned and what happened?' Facilitate discovery of causes — decisions made, information missing, coordination failures, unexpected conditions. The facilitator asks questions; participants surface the reasons. No blame is assigned.

steps.name: Identify what to sustain and what to improve
steps.description: Ask: 'What will we sustain and what will we improve?' Both questions matter equally. Participants name specific practices to keep and specific changes to make. The facilitator helps the team convert vague lessons ('communicate better') into concrete actions ('designate a decision log owner for the next event').

principles: Participants discover; the facilitator guides — conclusions imposed by a leader are not lessons learned, they are directives.
Rank is suspended — the soldier who saw the failure must be as free to speak as the general who ordered the action; otherwise the AAR produces the leader's version, not the truth.
Both successes and failures deserve an AAR — what went right is as worth understanding as what went wrong.
patterns.threshold: The team can name at least one specific practice they will sustain and one they will change, with a reason derived from the event — not a general resolve to 'do better' but a named behaviour tied to a named moment in the event.
patterns.masterpiece: A written lessons-learned record from the AAR that is specific enough to brief a team preparing for the next similar event — covering what was planned, what happened, why, and what concrete practices will be sustained or changed.

known_uses.name: US Army National Training Center
known_uses.quote: An AAR is a review of training that allows soldiers, leaders, and units to discover for themselves what happened during the training and why.
known_uses.note: FM 25-101: Battle Focused Training, Appendix G. Official Army doctrine definition. AAR institutionalised at National Training Center, Fort Irwin, from 1981.

known_uses.name: Software engineering retrospectives
known_uses.note: Sprint retrospectives in Scrum are a direct adaptation of AAR structure: what went well, what did not, what to change — same four questions in a software delivery context.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent training run reviews
known_uses.note: Post-episode structured review in agent training environments — what was the policy's intent, what trajectory resulted, what caused deviation, what parameter or prompt to adjust — mirrors the AAR four-question structure.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the leader assigns blame, rates performance, or announces conclusions rather than guiding participant discovery. A critique delivered in AAR format is not an AAR — it produces compliance, not learning, and shuts down the honest reporting that makes AARs valuable.

Anti-pattern: the AAR produces general resolve — 'we need to communicate better', 'coordination was an issue' — without naming specific behaviours to sustain or change. Vague lessons are not actionable and will not change the next performance.

patterns.ecosystem: military training, professional development, software engineering, autonomous-agent training

sources.title: FM 25-101: Battle Focused Training, Appendix G — After Action Reviews
sources.quote: An AAR is a review of training that allows soldiers, leaders, and units to discover for themselves what happened during the training and why.
sources.note: US Army Field Manual FM 25-101, September 1990. Appendix G. Official doctrinal source for AAR methodology.

sources.title: FM 25-101: Battle Focused Training, Glossary
sources.quote: a method of providing feedback to units by involving participants in the training diagnostic process in order to increase and reinforce learning. The AAR leader guides participants in identifying deficiencies and seeking solutions.
sources.note: FM 25-101 Glossary definition of After Action Review. Same manual, secondary definition confirming facilitated discovery framing.

sources.title: After Action Review: The Army's System for Learning from Every Experience
sources.note: Secondary source confirming AAR origin in post-Yom Kippur War analysis and institutionalisation at National Training Center (1981). The four questions: what was supposed to happen, what actually happened, why was there a difference, what to sustain or improve.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Significant event concludes
exercise / milestone / incident] --> B[AAR convened immediately
rank suspended]
    B --> C[Q1: What was planned?
establish shared baseline]
    C --> D[Q2: What actually happened?
multiple perspectives]
    D --> E[Q3: Why was there a gap?
facilitator questions, participants discover]
    E --> F[Q4: What to sustain and improve?
specific behaviours named]
    F --> G[Write lessons-learned record
actionable and specific]
    G --> H[More capable team
shared ownership of lessons]

patterns.example_scenario: A startup product team has just shipped a mobile feature that was three weeks late and required two emergency hotfixes in the first 48 hours. The engineering manager gathers the full team — designer, two engineers, QA, and the product manager — for an AAR the day after the hotfixes. She opens by stating the four questions they will work through, and says explicitly that she is not here to assign blame. Q1: The team agrees the plan was to ship in six weeks with one week of regression testing. Q2: Shipping took nine weeks; regression testing was compressed to two days. Q3: The engineers identify two causes: a dependency on a third-party SDK that behaved differently on older OS versions, discovered in week five; and a decision in week seven to skip a regression cycle to hit the milestone date. The product manager acknowledges making that call under schedule pressure. Q4: The team agrees to sustain: the early-week dependency check practice one engineer had started informally. To change: any decision to compress regression testing requires a documented trade-off, written and shared with the team before execution, not made by one person under pressure. The lessons-learned record is written and added to the team's engineering handbook. In the next release cycle, the trade-off document format is used twice, each time surfacing a risk that would otherwise have been invisible. The masterpiece is the lessons-learned record: specific enough to have changed the next release's process.
